Psychic Energy Reconsidered—Introduction

Robert Wallerstein M.D.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert S. Wallerstein, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry, Univ. Calif. San Francisco Langley Porter Neuropsychiatric Institute
San Francisco, California 94143

Robert Wallerstein  

ALTHOUGH THE CONCEPT OF PSYCHIC ENERGY IS one of the oldest concepts in the theory of psychoanalysis, it is still today one of the most obscure, most obfuscated, and most controversial. It was first enunciated by Freud as early as 1894 in his paper "The Neuro-Psychoses of Defence": "I should like, finally, to dwell for a moment on the working hypothesis which I have made use of in this exposition… I refer to the concept that in mental functions something is to be distinguished—a quota of affect or sum of excitation—which possesses all the characteristics of a quantity (though we have no means of measuring it), which is capable of increase, diminution, displacement and discharge, and which is spread over the memory-traces of ideas somewhat as an electric charge is spread over the surface of a body" (p. 60). Here, in this earliest statement, the energy concept, which in the later metapsychological papers became the core of the economic point of view, was linked to affect as its source. Quite soon, however, as Freud reconceptualized the nature of the pathogenic process in neurosis consequent upon his embarrassing and painful discovery that the ubiquitous sexual seductions called for in his original traumatic theory of the neuroses were, in fact, fantasy elaborations, he went on to develop an inner psychology of the vicissitudes of the instincts; with this development Freud made the drives

Book Notices

SUICIDE IN DIFFERENT CULTURES. Edited by Norman L. Farberow. Baltimore, Md.: University Park Press, 1975, 286 pp., $19.50.

The pioneer suicidologist Farberow has collected seventeen papers, including his own contributions, and organized them to present ethnological studies of the occurrence of suicide in nations from every quarter of the earth. Not all the contributors were native to the country on which they report. As Farberow and other suicidologists have indicated, however, reporting systems leave much to be desired. This is especially a problem when an interdicting political climate, let alone antithetical social and personal factors, prevail.

Would a modern psychoanalyst want to argue with Faberow's thesis that "in order to fully understand the self-determination of any individual, it is necessary to know the cultural matrix within which it occurs" or, that "culture will define and direct the way in which suicide occurs, is reacted to, and is reported in the countries of the world"? Yet, to fully understand what appears to be the nub of the book—"to provide the basis for evaluating how many suicidal phenomena are universal and how many reflect distinct, socio-cultural processes"—is also to assess developmental disturbance and intrapsychic conflict.

The final chapter, and one of the most cogent and enlivening to read, "Characteristics of Suicides and Attitudes Toward Suicide in Japan" by M. Iga and K. Tatai, is addressed to the uniqueness of Japanese suicides, and traces the life history and psychobiographical material in interpreting the Nobel prize winner Yukio Mishima's suicide as a disturbance of self- and object representation. Other intrinsic determinants for suicide are given in the statement of J. P. Webb and W. Willard that, despite the adaptive task of adjusting to new cultures, suicide among the Navajo "seems to be more related to family difficulties" than from a breakdown of Navajo culture.

[[RunningHead]]

Masturbation from Infancy to Senescence

Edited by Irwin M. Marcus and John J. Francis. New York: International Universities Press, 1975, 517 pp., $25.00

R. Dickinson M.D.

R. Dickinson

R. Hugh Dickinson, M.D.
Seattle, Washington

R. Dickinson  

This is a collection of 19 separate articles relating to the subject of masturbation. The differences in perspective of the various writers add interest to the book, and each of the papers makes a contribution of some value. Because, except for the editors' brief introduction, no attempt is made to integrate the essays, they may be read in random sequence. Although there may be some advantage to this, it is this reviewer's opinion that a valuable dimension would have been added had the editors included, perhaps a final summary or, better, brief interstitial comments. This would have made for a more cohesive text rather than an anthology on the subject.

The editors quote Freud's closing remarks to the Vienna Psychoanalytical Society's Conference on Masturbation in 1912: "You are aware of my preference for a fragmentary treatment of a subject …" and seem to have organized their book to follow this line—that is, to provide a collection of disconnected papers relating some more specifically and some more generally to masturbation. This format results, of course, in some inevitable repetition.

The book clarifies many of the terms related to masturbation. The longest and most extensive definition of masturbation itself is given by the editors in the opening article, wherein a distinction is made between "autoeroticism" and "autoerotic activity." The former term is used to describe a developmental stage, according to Nagera, the latter, to designate objectless instinctual activity providing pleasure from physical stimulation. When an object is involved in the activity, even in fantasy, it cannot be called "autoerotic activity.

Psychoanalysis of a Constipated Girl: Clinical Observations During the Fourth and Fifth Years

Jules Glenn M.D.

Jules Glenn

Jules Glenn, M.D.
21 Schenck Avenue
Great Neck, New York 11021

Jules Glenn  

THIS PAPER WILL SUMMARIZE the psychoanalysis of a girl who suffered from bowel-movement retention and will include the details of specific sessions of her analysis, which took place when she was three to four years old. This method of presentation has the advantage of permitting the reader access to the evidence on which psychoanalytic formulations are based, but the disadvantage of being but one case and a relatively brief analysis at that—one year in duration. Another difficulty is in simultaneously evaluating two interrelated sets of data: the complexities of the psychogenesis of the patient's illness and the inferences to be drawn concerning the psychology of girls her age. In studying psychopathology, we may also see normal development in relief. Conflicts, wishes, fantasies, and defenses are communicated to us vividly as only a patient eager for help can communicate them.

Clinical Associate Professor, Division of Psychoanalytic Education, Downstate Medical Center, State University of New York.

This paper is a modified version of a paper presented at the Panel on the Psychology of Women: Infancy and Early Childhood at the May, 1974 meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

Submitted August 15, 1975

The Significance of the Trifurcation of the Road: Clinical Confirmation and Illumination

Jerome Weinberger M.D. and Jules Glenn M.D.

Jules Glenn

Jerome L. Weinberger, M.D.
Jules Glenn, M.D.
51 Prattle Street
Cambridge, Mass., 02138

Jules Glenn  

SUMMARY

Our clinical material form the analyses of two patients confirms Abraham's hypothesis that the bifurcation of the road symbolizes the female genitals. This adds credence to his suggestion that the site at which Oedipus killed Laius, according to the Greek myth, represents the mother's genitals over which father and son do battle. In addition, we suggest that the junction of the torso and the lower extremities, the location of the genitals, is a sensory base not only for the symbol of the bifurcation of the road, but also for the symbol three. The fork in the road depicts uncertainty about arriving at a decision, ascertaining whether to select an incestuous or nonincestuous object, a heterosexual or homosexual object.

Contemporary Sexual Behavior: Critical Issues in the 1970's.

Edited by Joseph Zubin and John Money. Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 1973, xx + 468 pp., $15.00.

Vann Spruiell M.D.

Vann Spruiell

Vann Spruiell, M.D.
New Orleans, Louisiana

Vann Spruiell  

The compleat psychoanalyst, looking around for ideas, may turn to a collection of papers presented at the 61st annual meeting of the American Psychopathological Association. It is doubtful that the psychoanalyst will be pleased. Here and there he may come upon some food for thought. But in other places he is apt to find—depending on his temperament—more fuel for anger, sorrow, or derision. Biology, ethology, animal and human psychology, neural science, sociology, pharmacology, and psychiatry are all represented, and so is psychoanalysis—in one lone, admirable paper by Robert Stoller. A wide range of approaches, one would think; nevertheless, in the preface the editors apologize for the fact that practicality did not allow inclusion of all the sexual issues of the 1970's. Among those left out is any consideration of love.

Nothing much holds the papers in this collection together, and the result is a kind of potpourri. The tendency to eschew any sort of general psychological theory (or even biological theory) is countered only by Stoller, the psychoanalyst. Some of the papers are narrow in scope but rigorous in the presentation of data. Others are global, loosely pontifical, or faddishly political. A number, including several of the otherwise scientific papers, contain gratuitous attacks on psychoanalysis in general and Freud's person in particular. That these attacks are simplistic and ignorant is comforting in one way and distressing in another. It is comforting to note that they represent prejudices rather than substantial criticism; most of the reports dealing with data rather than mere opinion can be integrated into psychoanalysis as a general psychology with little or no difficulty. It is distressing, however, that psychoanalysis has been so incapable of making itself understood by sizable numbers of other scientists.

One reason for examining this book, then, is to winnow out some of the contributions from other disciplines that actually have relevance for psychoanalysis. Another is to consider a pseudoscientific dogma that has enthralled colleagues in other disciplines. [[RunningHead]]

Psychic Change in Psychoanalysis

Charles Brenner M.D. and Allan Compton M.D.

Allan Compton

Charles Brenner, M.D.
Allan Compton, M.D.
3655 Lomita Boulevard
Torrance, California 90505

Allan Compton  

Dr. Charles Brenner, the Chairman, noted the broad potential of the chosen topic and the intention of the panel to discuss change in many senses: as a philosophic concept, leading to considerations of the relation between psychoanalysis and physical science; as oscillation about a baseline of forward progress during clinical analysis, leading to recommendations concerning interpretation and technique; as alteration of psychic conflicts; and in relation to problems in devising methods for assessment of psychic changes resulting from the psychoanalytic process. The panel had previously decided not to read extended prepared papers, but rather to make brief opening statements, to be expanded as appropriate in the course of the discussion.

Robert Michels traced the origin of concepts of change outside of and long before psychoanalysis. These concepts have influenced and perhaps constricted psychoanalytic thinking about change within analysis. Psychoanalysis, so far as it is a method of treatment, is concerned with change, with having an impact upon patients treated, despite some concern about the appropriateness for analysts of a focus on change during treatment. Aristotle distinguished four types of change: in quantity, in place, in quality, and change in which nothing persists, which he called "generation and corruption." The last type was viewed as instantaneous, in contrast to the first three—forms of motion—which took place over time. Change, for Aristotle, implied a potential that became actualized rather than a continuous series of shifting actualities.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York City, December, 1976. Panelists: Robert Michels and Herbert J. Schlesinger.

[[RunningHead]]

La Dépression Et Les États-Limites

By Jean Bergeret. Paris: Payot, 1974, 354 pp.

David Terman M.D.

David Terman

David Terman  

Psychoanalytic exploration of the phenomena of narcissism and the theoretical reformulations that study has engendered have stimulated both controversy and growth. The shape of our science is

260

changing, and the present period of revision and disagreement is part of the process of growth.

Jean Bergeret, a French psychoanalyst from Lyon with considerable clinical experience, offers us another exposition and formulation of these phenomena in his recent work, Depression and Borderline States. His book is divided into three sections. The first spells out his clinical theory, the second outlines a nosology based on that theory, and the third elucidates his clinical approach.

In the theoretical section, the author offers the notion that there are three distinct lines of development—psychotic, neurotic, and borderline. Using Fleiss's ontogenetic table, he assigns a libidinal fixation point to each and holds that the nature of the subsequent evolution is determined by the nature of the psychic apparatus at the point of fixation. The psychotic line of development is quite stable. It stems from a very early (oral to anal-expulsive) fixation when the ego is not yet whole. The psychotic line of development essentially is an evolution of a fragmented ego. Such an evolution can produce either a stable character type, which he calls the psychotic character, or an unstable decompensation of the character organization, a psychosis. This psychotic organization, whether stable or in decompensation, is always characterized by these attributes: denial of reality, primacy of narcissistic libido and the pleasure principle, decathexis of objects, and various projective phenomena.

The neurotic line of development, on the other hand, takes it origin and structure from a full oedipal experience. That experience must include libidinal investment in genitality and the triangular object relations and conflicts resulting from those instinctual vicissitudes. The consequent psychological structure includes a whole ego with repression as the principal defense. The nuclear structure is fully cathected oedipal conflict, and there is an effective functioning superego formed as the result of its resolution. The later adult character structure is called "neurotic," its decompensation is neurosis.

La Sexualité Perverse: Études Psychoanalytiques

By Ilse and Robert Barande, Joyce McDougall, Michel de M'Uzan, Christian David, René Major, and Sidney Stewart. Paris: Payot, 1972, 256 pp., 29.70F.

James Naiman M.D.

James Naiman

James Naiman, M.D.
Montreal

James Naiman  

The authors of this book are Paris psychoanalysts interested in the subject of perversions and sharing certain ideas. Substantial disagreements between them, including disagreement on whether or not there is a specific dynamic structure in perversions, precluded them from writing a book under joint authorship, so that what we are presented with is in fact a collection of individual papers. Two of these (those by De M'Uzan and by McDougall) have appeared in English in the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis.1

De M'Uzan's papers is based on two extensive interviews with a 65-year-old man who was referred by a radiologist because of evidence of severe past tortures on the patient's body. The right breast had been burned with a red-hot iron, molten lead had been introduced into the navel, the end of the penis had been cut open with a razor to make the orifice larger, and a steel ring had been placed there permanently. He also had numerous tattoos of a humiliating nature. The patient had married a fifteen-year-old cousin when he was 25. She was a masochist like himself. Tortures were inflicted on them by one or two other men who played the sadistic role. For the first three years they had a normal sexual life.

[[RunningHead]]

Sexual Identity Conflict in Children and Adults.

By Richard Green. New York: Basic Books, 1974, 352 pp., $15.00.

Lester Schwartz M.D.

Lester Schwartz

Lester Schwartz, M.D.
New York City

Lester Schwartz  

This book attempts to present an overview of the complexities of sexual identity with particular emphasis on transsexualism. Green defines sexual identity or gender identity as (1) an individual's basic conviction of being male or female, (2) his behavior culturally associated with masculinity or femininity, and (3) preference for male or female sexual partners. Transsexualism, in this context, refers to an individual with a persisting wish to change sex.

In Green's presentation, the predominant mode of exposition is the vignette of clinical interview—with the mixture of the dramatic and the oversimplified implicit in that approach. His orientation is essentially nonpsychoanalytic; the emphasis is on behavioral manifestations, on simple learning theory schemata, with a deliberate exclusion of fantasy life, mental states, or the unconscious.

The books is introduced by sections devoted to historical and cross-cultural surveys, psychological theories, and biological studies. The theme of sexual transformation is shown to be universal in human culture. Biological studies seem to demonstrate that, in animals, prenatal sex hormone administration influences postnatal sexual behavior. Indications are that in humans, there are no correlations between hormonal levels and sexual identity, although there have been some suggestions that heightened androgen levels may correlate with increased masculine behavior in girls. The author stresses the difficulties implicit in evaluating studies in this area. In any event, there is no support offered for those who would view sexual identity as other than the result of complex interactions of physical, psychological, and social factors. The review of psychological theories, after a survey of early Freudian concepts, for the most part follows Robert Stoller (who provides the foreword to this book).

707

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

A PRINCE OF OUR DISORDER. By John E. Mack. Boston: Little, Brown, 1976, 537 pp., $15.00.

This is an extraordinarily clearly written and readable biography of Lawrence of Arabia. The author, a psychoanalyst and also an expert historian, has devoted more than a decade of research to gathering his data. Probably nowhere else is so much information about Lawrence to be found. Among the new sources used are interviews with desert tribesmen who provided eyewitness accounts of events that have been doubted by critics. The son of the Turkish officer who was involved in the sexual assault on Lawrence at Derá was questioned about his father's sex life. The psychoanalytic reader will be aware that Mack has kept his psychological explanations simple, the book having apparently been intended for the general public. Except in rare instances, psychological information is not documented by specific references to the literature. Mack tends to isolate certain aspects of Lawrence's life without attempting to tie them in with the total field of his existence. The author states that "some qualities in all of us must stand by themselves without explanation." With the rule of psychic determinism thus suspended, areas of Lawrence's life are exempt from psychological conjectures. Fortunately, this attitude is not all pervasive. The book is essentially a biography, with the psychoanalytic contributions concentrated principally in the Introduction, Chapter 19, page 321, and Chapters 33 and 34. The author states that his purpose is to offer a biographical study of a "public" figure who lives out elements of his inner life in the public area. Mack believes that, if data about his inner life were obtainable, information about the psychology of action could be developed.

725

[[RunningHead]]

A Case Study of Embarrassment

Oliver Dann M.D.

Oliver Dann

Oliver T. Dann, M.D.
Veterans Administration Hospital
West Haven, Conn. 06516

Oliver Dann  

SUMMARY

The psychoanalytic references to embarrassment are reviewed. Embarrassment, in the literature, is seen largely as an affect involving exhibitionistic and scopophilic conflicts and defenses against these.

A case in which embarrassment was prominent is discussed. Embarrassment

467

in the patient was an ego response which implied an external object for its manifestation. It involved exhibitionistic and scopophilic conflicts and projective defenses, but also operated in ego-gratifying and adaptive ways. Her embarrassment was understood through the analysis of an initial embarrassing dream of nakedness and other dreams and associated material as the defensive outgrowth of repeated exposures to the primal scene. Embarrassment was a resistance to remembering in the analysis, and the primal-scene experiences were partially reconstructed. The analytic situation was, in many ways, a symbolic re-creation of the primal scene, including the patient's response of embarrassment. The development of embarrassment in the patient's childhood was furthered and confirmed by its being an identification with the attitudes of both parents.

Finally, some reflections on embarrassment and shame in its various forms are set forth.

[[RunningHead]]

A Literary Footnote to "Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety"

William Jeffrey M.D.

William Jeffrey

William D. Jeffrey, M.D.
29 8th Avenue
Brooklyn, New York 11217

William Jeffrey  

IN THE DISCUSSION OF ANIMAL PHOBIAS in "Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety," besides the well-known cases of Little Hans and the Wolf Man, Freud (1926) briefly mentions a third patient, a young American. The American as a child "had been sexually excited by a fantastic children's story … about an Arab chief who pursued a 'ginger-bread man' so as to eat him up. He identified himself with this edible person, and the Arab chief was easily recognizable as a father substitute" (p. 105). The book is not referenced.

L. Frank Baum, the author of The Wonderful Wizard of Oz(1900) and the popular Oz sequels, wrote a lesser known American fairy tale John Dough and the Cherub in 1906. John Dough is the vulnerable gingerbread man who eluded the voracious Arab. Not only does the unusual plot make it clear that this is the story that impressed the young American, but its publication in America antidated Freud's book by twenty years. This would be time enough for the child to mature and enter into treatment with Freud.

The Arab is indeed the father to the gingerbread man; his most precious possession, the Great Elixir, is inadvertently mixed with gingerbread batter and produces life in John Dough.

873

[[RunningHead]]

Ambiguity in Speech

Richard Isay M.D.

Richard Isay

Richard A. Isay, M.D.
100 York Street
New Haven, Connecticut 06511

Richard Isay  

SUMMARY

Ambiguity in speech during analysis is one modality for the expression of unconscious conflict and one manifestation of transference. Four types of ambiguity are illustrated. Each ambiguity communicates an unconscious wish or intention and the defense against this wish or intention. In each type of ambiguity, transference manifestations are expressed in the form of speech and not alone by its content, as we are accustomed

449

to identifying the transference. Each type of ambiguity within the analysis expresses fear of responsibility and recrimination for libidinal or aggressive intentions. In some patients, the combinative ambiguity or malapropism expresses the fear of reprisal through the transformation into a self-inflicted injury. The pronominal ambiguity may express a feared and wished-for fusion with the analyst. The analyst's ambiguous interpretations are properly used to encourage associations, but at times may reflect lack of understanding and be a manifestation of countertransference.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis, Folklore, and Processes of Socialization

L. Boyer M.D. and Daniel Freeman M.D.

Daniel Freeman

L. Bryce Boyer, M.D.
Daniel M.A. Freeman, M.D.
721 Upsal Road
Jenkintown, Pennsylvania 19046

Daniel Freeman  

The format of this panel derived from its cross-cultural and interdisciplinary nature. L. Bryce Boyer provided background by reviewing early interdisciplinary efforts in applied psychoanalysis and the evolution of the psychoanalytic approach to the understanding of folklore. Ruth M. Boyer then presented and discussed a group of Apache myths, which served as the focus for the panelists' contributions.

Bryce Boyer reviewed the collaborative efforts of psychoanalysts, anthropologists, and folklorists. Early attempts at applied psychoanalysis sought to understand expressive culture through the use of Freud's new insights concerning the Unconscious, but found intense disapprobation from social scientists of other disciplines. Some few anthropologists and folklorists were grateful that Freud, Abraham, Rank, and others made the psychological aspects of folklore and religion understandable in terms of the language of the dream. However, even those who granted that there were psychological aspects to expressive culture were affronted by a prevalent circular reasoning. When their thinking was dominated by the topographical viewpoint, many analysts sought to use the study of folklore to demonstrate the validity of their recently acquired knowledge concerning unconscious mechanisms. The topographical hypothesis facilitated arbitrary symbolic interpretations, which early analysts employed to define new latent meanings and to reinterpret myths.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Baltimore, May, 1976.

Panelists: Ruth M. Boyer, Alan Dundes, Daniel M. A. Freeman, Jacob A. Arlow.

Submitted: June 29, 1976

1Arlow, J. A. (1961), Ego Psychology and the Study of Mythology. This Journal, 5:371-393.235

Marvin Starman  

THE PRESENTATION ITSELF centered around a series of movie sequences—and appropriate illustrative case material either to emphasize or further elaborate data pertinent to understanding the child being observed—which highlight certain trends in the development from early infancy through early school years. This was combined with further follow-up material through the subject's twenty-seventh year of life.

As Dr. Fries presented the film material, she invited the audience to formulate questions, especially regarding predictions one would make from the data; for example: Would the child develop a neurosis? What defenses would develop from the material seen? Would the patient be motivated toward marriage, motherhood, etc.?

At this point, Jacob Arlow also encouraged those participating to consider predictions regarding the development of the child; for example: identificatory elements, defensive type, and what form of neurosis or character structure might we expect. How would the ego autonomy be affected, etc.?

David Freedman, who has conducted research in the development of blind children, offered some prepared comments.

[[RunningHead]]

La Faim Et Le Corps

By Evelyne Kestemberg et al. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France, 1972, 301 pp., $15.00

Francis Baudry M.D.

Francis Baudry

Francis Baudry, M.D.
New York City

Francis Baudry  

It is not easy to review this book on anorexia nervosa by Kestemberg et al. The problems presented to this reviewer fall into three distinct categories, each deserving separate treatment: (1) the ambiguous nature of the disease; (2) the treatment method chosen by the authors—at variance with the classical analytic situation; and (3) the authors' basic theoretical orientation, which pressures them into a certain format of interpretation.

The authors' stated goal is not to construct a new metapsychological formulation of this illness or to arrive at a specific psychoanalytic constellation of its dynamics. Rather, their initial concern is clinical: how can it happen that, starting with the child proclaiming he is going to stop eating to punish his mother (indicating an incomplete differentiation between him and her), certain people go as far as starving to death? The authors came to conclude, to their great surprise, that on the road to death, the children "died of pleasure." This discovery led to the deepening of certain clinical and metapsychological concepts—perversion and primary masochism.

The first chapter, a historical survey, will serve as a backdrop for the discussion of the first set of problems dealing with the basic nature of this disease entity. In general, the development in the understanding of this illness from an analytic point of view has followed that of analytic theory, with each successive advance being applied to it in the hope of clarifying the picture (object-relations theory, adolescent asceticism, separation trauma, drug addiction, theories of development of self, etc.). The diagnostic difficulty resides in separating a specific disease entity from all the so-called nervous anorexias secondary to either hysterical or psychotic disorders.

[[RunningHead]]

A Critique of Psychic Energy as an Explanatory Concept

Don Swanson Ph.D.

Don Swanson

Don R. Swanson, Ph.D.
Graduate Library School University of Chicago
1100 East 57 Street
Chicago, Illinois 60637

Don Swanson  

SUMMARY

Psychic-energy, libido, and related concepts in Freud's drive-discharge theory are either impossible, useless, or mistaken. If they are defined in a mind-body interactionist framework,

629

they are impossible in the sense that they violate energy-conservation laws of physics. If they are defined in any abstract framework, they are useless in that explanations of behavior so based are trivially and incorrigibly circular. If they are defined instead as biological concepts, the implied notions of energy flow in neural networks are mistaken; nevertheless, the biological framework seems most reasonable and helpful to understanding what Freud was attempting with his energy-discharge theory. I have not attempted to examine Freud's clinical theory, as contrasted with his drive-discharge theory; I refer to the distinction drawn by Klein (1976).

[[RunningHead]]

Boundaries and Structure in Love Relations

Otto Kernberg M.D.

Otto Kernberg

Otto F. Kernberg, M.D.
21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains, New York 10605

Otto Kernberg  

Summary and Reformulation of Previous Work

MY WORK WITH PATIENTS PRESENTING BORDERLINE personality organization, particularly those with narcissistic personality structure, suggested that a patient's capacity to fall in love and to establish lasting love relationships had important diagnostic and prognostic implications (1974a), (1974b). In exploring these findings, I concluded that the capacity to fall and to remain in love reflected the successful completion of two developmental stages: In the first stage, the early capacity for sensuous stimulation of erotogenic zones is integrated with the later capacity for establishing a total object relation. In the second stage, full genital enjoyment incorporates early body-surface eroticism in the context of a total object relation, including a complementary sexual identification. The first stage requires, in essence, that primitive dissociation or lack of integration of the self and of object representations be overcome in the context of establishing ego identity and the capacity for object relations in depth. The second stage requires the successful overcoming of oedipal conflicts and the related unconscious prohibitions against a full sexual relation.

Medical Director, New York Hospital-Cornell Medical Center, Westchester Division, White Plains, New York; Professor of Psychiatry, Cornell University Medical College, New York, New York.

Submitted April 5, 1976

The Psychoanalytic Treatment of Male Homosexuality

George Wiedeman M.D. and Edmund Payne M.D.

Edmund Payne

George H. Wiedeman, M.D.
Edmund C. Payne, M.D.
66 Fountain Street
West Newton, Msssachusetts 02165

Edmund Payne  

In introducing the panel, George Wiedeman noted that it has been more than ten years since the American Psychoanalytic Association has sponsored a panel discussion related to homosexuality and psychoanalysis. This panel deals with the psychoanalytic treatment of patients whoses preference for homosexual erotic relationships is part of their personality structure. In recognition of the attacks that some activist groups have directed against the psychoanalytic and psychiatric investigation of homosexuality, Wiedeman reminded the audience that the psychoanalysis of homosexuals, as in any analytic treatment, is predicated on the voluntary agreement by the prospective patients to undertake analysis. Though we cannot forsee whether the change from homosexuality to heterosexuality will occur, such a change occurs often enough to warrant analysis for those who are conflicted and unhappy about their homosexuality. Analysts have always opposed any archaic and barbaric laws directed against homosexuals, as well as any other forms of social or political discrimination against them. In fact, certain deeply entrenched hostile attitudes against homosexuals have been undermined by the results of psychoanalytic research.

In the first presentation, Robert J. Stoller observed that we already have good theory and data on male homosexuality, due especially to the work of Irving Bieber and Socarides who have elaborated Freud's original studies on the origins of homosexuality in childhood.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Baltimore, May, 1976.

Panelists: Ethel S. Person, Charles W. Socarides, Robert J. Stoller.

Submitted July 9, 1976

Normal and Pathological Nostalgia

David Werman M.D.

David Werman

David S. Werman, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry The University of North Carolina
Chapel Hill, North Carolina 27514

David Werman  

SUMMARY

Nostalgia is distinguished from homesickness from which it was originally derived, and from fantasy to which it is related. It is described as an affective-cognitive experience, usually involving memories of places in one's past. These memories are associated with a characteristic affective coloration described as "bittersweet." It is concluded that the locales remembered are displacements from objects whose representation was repressed. Nostalgia is a ubiquitous human experience that is evoked by particular stimuli under special circumstances, and, while it is generally a normal occurrence, pathological

397

forms occur. Among those discussed are: nostalgia as a substitute for mourning, as an attempted mastery through idealization and displacement of a painful past, as a resistance in analysis, and as a counterphobic mechanism. Nostalgia not only serves as a screen memory, but may also be said to operate as a screen affect.

George Pollock  

OUR ASSOCIATION TRADITIONALLY OFFERS its immediate Past-President the opportunity to speak to the membership and its guests on a topic of his choosing. What he selects to talk about usually reflects his experiences and what he has learned from them. Some of our Past-Presidents have made scientific presentations, some have focused on the problems, issues, and concerns that confronted them, and others have reported on the state of organized psychoanalysis. During my two years as President-Elect and President, I have had many experiences, some gratifying and others disappointing. In the months since our last meeting, I have attempted to consider some of these experiences from a broader psychoanalytic ordering framework, and today I want to share some of my thoughts and insights with you.

A science, in order to remain vital and renewed, is obligated to apply its principles and methodology to itself whenever possible. Our science, even though it deals primarily with the individual, has many principles that can be utilized in understanding some of our organizational issues.

Plenary Session Post-Presidential Address, given December 20, 1975, at the Midwinter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association in New York.

[[RunningHead]]

The Question of Proof in Freud's Psychoanalytic Writings

Paul Ricoeur

Paul Ricoeur

Paul Ricoeur
Divinity School, Swift Hall The University of Chicago
1025 East 58 Street
Chicago, Illinois 60637

Paul Ricoeur  

THE QUESTION OF PROOF in psychoanalysis is as old as psychoanalysis itself. The 1895 "Project" aims at being a project of scientific psychology. The "Interpretation of Dreams" purports to be a science and not a fantastic construction, a "fine fairy tale," to use Krafft-Ebing's remark, hurled at Freud at the close of one public presentation. Each of Freud's didactic works—the Introductory Lectures, the New Introductory Lectures, and the Outline—represent a new effort to communicate to the layman the conviction that psychoanalysis is genuinely related to what is intelligible and what claims to be true. And yet, psychoanalysis has never quite succeeded in stating how its assertions are justified, how its interpretations are authenticated, how its theory is verified. This relative failure of psychoanalysis to be recognized as a science results, I think, from a failure to ask certain preliminary questions to which I devote the first two parts of my paper; the third part is an attempt to reply directly to the original question.

The Criteria for "Facts" in Psychoanalysis

The first question concerns what is relevant as a fact in psychoanalysis.

The Psychoanalytic Understanding of Narcissistic and Borderline Personality Disorders: Advances in Developmental Theory

Calvin Settlage M.D.

Calvin Settlage

Calvin F. Settlage, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry Mt. Iion Hospital
1600 Divisadero Street
San Francisco, California 94115

Calvin Settlage  

SUMMARY

I have reviewed and supported the proposition that theory building in psychoanalysis requires the careful correlation and meaningful interpenetration of the data and hypotheses from both the reconstructive and direct observational approaches. In illustration of this premise, I postulated that failure to achieve age-adequate self-regulation and ego autonomy during the rapprochement subphase of the separation-individuation process contributes crucially to the pathogenesis of

830

narcissistic and borderline disorders. I have also described the rapprochement subphase of the separation-individuation process and attempted to draw some correlations between it and the psychological formations characteristic of narcissistic personality disorders. Clinical material from the analyses of a three-year-old girl and an eighteen-year-old girl was employed to demonstrate the genetic origin of such pathology and to illustrate some facets of analytic process in the treatment of a narcissistic personality disorder. Lastly, I noted and supported the controversial concepts of structure formation as a result of psychoanalytic treatment, and of analytic process as being both therapeutic and developmental. In these attempts, I have been viewing mostly familiar concepts and clinical experience mainly from the perspective provided by advances in the psychoanalytic theory of early psychic development.

The Interpretation of Transference and the Conditions for Loving

Roy Schafer Ph.D.

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer, Ph.D.
525 East 68 Street
New York, New York 10021

Roy Schafer  

THE PROGRESS OF THE DISCIPLINE OF PSYCHOANALYSIS is expressed perhaps most obviously in its theory of transference and the therapeutic effects of the interpretation of transference. The theory of transference rests on assumptions concerning repetition and regression or the influence of the past on the present; the roles of activity, passivity, and defensive measures; and the content of unconscious fantasy and conflict. The theory of the therapeutic effects of interpretation rests on these same assumptions and on others concerning the nature of insight, the role of the therapeutic relationship, the accessibility of unconscious fantasy and conflict to conscious influence, and the balance in the psychoanalytic process between, on the one hand, reliving and re-experiencing of the past and, on the other, new experience.

Clearly, the topic is vast, and one cannot hope to do justice in any single discussion to all the valuable contributions to the extensive literature on it; nor can one cover all that is widely understood and accepted in ordinary good practice. In this paper I shall limit myself to re-examining some of the assumptions I just mentioned, doing so from the standpoint of the revised mode of psychoanalytic conceptualization I call action language. Insofar as I have presented the premises and rules of this "new language for psychoanalysis" elsewhere at some length (Schafer, 1976), I shall not dwell on them here.

A modified version of the twenty-first Rado Lecture, presented before the Association for Psychoanalytic Medicine, April 1, 1976.

From the Payne Whitney Clinic of the New York Hospital-Cornell Medical Center.

[[RunningHead]]

Ambivalence, Passion, and Love

Therese Benedek M.D.

Therese Benedek

Therese Benedek, M.D.
180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, Illinois 60601

Therese Benedek  

SUMMARY

This essay spans the range from the fundamental concepts of psychoanalytic theory to the epigenetic phenomena of sociocultural processes that involve the relationship between the sexes.

Ambivalence is defined as the psychic representation of the two poles of instinctual energy: active = positive, passive = negative. Sexual drive, the psychic representation of the sexual instinct, is potentially ambivalent. It is assumed that an ambivalent emotional state, experienced by primitive men, means danger from within, activates defense against the sexual drive; thus the primary repression of the sexual drive precedes the evolution of conflict. The psychological consequence of primary ambivalence is emotional ambivalence that involves ambivalent attitudes toward the object of the drive. The primary instinctual conflicts are examined in the

77

frame of prehistoric civilizations: the cult of the Magna Mater and totemism.

The concept of the primary ambivalence of the sexual drive is derived from the active, extraverted, male sexual drive. The primary instinctual ambivalence of women cannot be attributed to the passive sexual drive; it is rooted in the female procreative psychobiology. The positive tendency and wish for childbearing and motherhood are opposed by the fear of pregnancy and death.

Passion is the intensification of affects that results from a spiral of interaction of the positive and negative poles of the instinct. Sexual passion involves reciprocal object cathexes that become the measure of the self-esteem of the lovers.

The primary instinctual ambivalence is a genic characteristic of each sex. The "polarity of the sexes" is remote from the source of the primary ambivalence. Rooted in the genic constitution of the sex, the polarity of the sexes is an epigenetic phenomenon, molded by the mutual influence of sexuality and culture. Castration fear and penis envy are the focal conflicts that express the ambivalence. Evolved in the process of ameliorating the primary ambivalence, the polarity functions to actuate sexual attraction and raise sexual tension. The process of love is described in the adaptation between marital partners. The idealizing, sexual love becomes a lasting bond through mutual identifications between husband and wife.

The Quantitative Line of Approach in Psychoanalysis: A Clinical Assessment of its Current Status

Milton Horowitz M.D.

Milton Horowitz

Milton H. Horowitz, M.D.
11 E. 68 Street
New York, N. Y. 10021

Milton Horowitz  

SUMMARY

The metapsychological points of view were designed by Freud, using the vocabulary of the science of his time, to provide a

577

model of the mental apparatus based on the data of conflict. This model, like other primitive scientific models, was based upon metaphor. One of those metaphors was a quantification concept; in the absence of any specific thing to measure, it presented a niche in the structure for future dimensional quantification; moreover, developmental issues of structure formation may require further quantitative concepts. The economic metaphors ("displaceable quantity") serve some usefulness in the model of personal history in which events of the past achieve significance in the present.

The dynamic and economic viewpoints contribute a model of a stylized segment of experience defined by the historical method. It is a model that may not be suitable for other exploratory methods. It may not stand up to other exploratory methods, which would require their own theory. This does not mean that it need be discarded, any more than we would discard Newtonian mechanics. We would simply regard the theory as limited to its observational method, and it could be retained in clinical theory as explanatory of symptom formation, interpretation of dream content, the theory of the self, etc. As in other branches of medicine, the case history is an essential beginning of clinical investigation; it cannot lead to precise etiological formulations and unitary explanations.

[[RunningHead]]

Some Vicissitudes of Love

Leon Altman M.D.

Leon Altman

Leon L. Altman, M.D.
1 West 64 Street
New York, New York 10023

Leon Altman  

WHAT IS LOVE? FOR MOST OF MANKIND it has been a sweet mystery whose presence was a miracle, whose absence was a disaster. For philosophers, before and after Plato, love has been the subject of serious disquisition and debate as either madness or the only road to salvation. To poets over the ages, it is reason for ecstasy, dismay, or serious perplexity. Love has its undoubted biology, its possible chemistry, its manifest physiology, and what may be deemed its anatomy. But it had no scientific psychology until Freud began his investigations into the depths of human behavior.

One may trace the evolution of Freud's thinking on love from the beginning in the Fliess letters (1892-1899) to the end in the Outline (1940). Hitschmann (1952) took the trouble to ascertain that Freud discussed love not less than 130 times in his writings.

Since 1939 there have been contributions dealing with type of object choice (Reich, 1953), genital primacy (Panel, 1969), the development of object relations (Modell, 1968), but not very many articles directed to the subject of love in its own right. Balint (1960), attributing a primordial constitutional aptitude—a diathesis—to love, offers a conception of primary love whose aim is to "establish—or, probably, re-establish—an all-embracing harmony with one's environment, to be able to love in peace" (p. 36). That he uses the word love within his definitions reinforces the impression that he considers love a given. Josselyn (1971)

[[RunningHead]]

Energy, Information, and Motivation: A Revision of Psychoanalytic Theory

Allan Rosenblatt M.D. and James Thickstun M.D.

James Thickstun

Allan D. Rosenblatt, M.D.
James T. Thickstun, M.D.
1200 Prospect Street
La Jalla, California 92037

James Thickstun  

IT IS, BY NOW, A TRUISM IN THE HISTORY of science that a demonstrably defective scientific paradigm is only abandoned when a competing or substitute paradigm is accepted by the scientific community. In a 1970 paper we were only able to sketch an alternative theoretical framework, involving information processing theory, concept and symbol formation, and current neurophysiological data. However, in the intervening years, our ideas and those of others have become more crystallized. Therefore, today, rather than merely repeat the same criticisms of the concept of psychic energy, we shall summarize these criticisms and then go on to focus upon a more coherent alternative conceptual framework.

The theory of psychic energy fails to meet the minimal criteria of acceptable scientific methodology in six major areas: (1) There is internal contradiction and lack of consistency; (2) it is a logically closed system, misusing metaphor as fact; (3) it uses tautological renaming to masquerade as explanation; (4) it cannot explain all relevant data; (5) it leads to a false sense of explanation; and (6) it promotes a mind-body dualism, preventing articulation with related sciences and scientific data.

[[RunningHead]]

The Significance of the Colors Black and White in the Dreams of Black and White Patients

Wayne Myers M.D.

Wayne Myers

Wayne A. Myers, M.D.
1385 York Avenue [Apt. 17G]
New York, N.Y. 10021

Wayne Myers  

SUMMARY

Dreams from three black patients and dreams from three white patients in analysis and in psychotherapy were analyzed with respect to spontaneous references to the colors black and white.

With the black patients I have worked with in interracial analysis, problems in self-object differentiation and in the development of the sense of identity, as well as intrapsychic conflicts, have come to be unconsciously organized in accord with meanings ascribed to the contrasting colors black and white. Identifications with parental conflicts structured along such color lines, when reinforced by reality experiences, tend to foster the development of ambivalent splitting of self- and object representations of the degraded and idealized varieties, in line with values attached to the colors black and white. This

180

ambivalent splitting is reactivated in the transference in interracial analysis and often serves as an intense resistance, most often clearly manifest in dreams and fantasies where the colors black and white appear. The colors black and white show changing connotations during the course of the analysis.

Emphasis is paid to the depiction, via the colors black and white, of contrasting moods, such as depression-elation and rage-calmness. In addition, the use of these colors as defensive camouflage is noted. Similar relevance to these colors is observable in the white-patient control group.

The colored dream screen inferred in black patients also may be seen in white patients, as wishful object representations of the good mother-breast may be associated with mother surrogates of either color.

Longitudinal Study: Prenatal Period to Parenthood

Margaret Fries M.D.

Margaret Fries

Margaret E. Fries, M.D.
103 East 86 Street
New York, New York 10028

Margaret Fries  

MANY YEARS AGO I ATTEMPTED TO STUDY and isolate the role played by the infant's constitution in its own future psychic life (Fries, 1935). It was customary in 1925 for the infant, after discharge from the hospital, to be seen first by the pediatrician in the Well Baby Clinic. We were surprised, in 1931, to find that an eight-week-old infant was too old for this study, inasmuch as mother and infant had already established mutual patterns, so that the contribution of each could not be distinguished (Fries, 1935). It was clear that the environment had already affected the infant's innate personality disposition. My investigation, therefore, would have to start with knowledge of the conscious and unconscious attitudes of the pregnant mother in order to isolate the infant's constitution, which is why we studied the family during the mother's pregnancy (Fries and Lewi, 1938), (Fries, 1941a).

The research into constitutional factors was carried out on 200 neonates around the clock from birth to the tenth day (Fries, 1937). Only in this way could a proper study of the interaction of mother and neonate be made so that constitutional and developmental factors could be distinguished.

Work focused on two areas: (1)

This paper amplified by film clips of children from birth to seven years, constituted the basis for a Discussion Group at the Fall Meeting of The American Psychoanalytic Association in 1973, under the title "Longitudinal Study from Prenatal to Adulthood." Dr. Fries introduced her film and described one of the four longitudinal cases shown in the film and reported in this paper. A report of the discussion follows.

Submitted May 20, 1975

Nonverbal Aspects of Child and Adult Psychoanalysis

Robert Wallerstein M.D. and Roy Lilleskov M.D.

Roy Lilleskov

Robert Wallerstein, M.D.
Roy K. Lilleskov, M.D.
55 East 87th Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

Roy Lilleskov  

Dr. Robert Wallerstein, after welcoming the audience and introducing the panelists, observed that adult analysis had originated as a "talking cure" and child analysis began with an emphasis on unraveling the meaning of play. Yet the dichotomy was not distinct because in child analysis the focus on nonverbal behavior was as a mode of access and understanding, necessitated by the immaturity of the child and meant to be converted to verbalization in order to promote insight. And though Freud stressed the resistance aspect of repetition of behavior in his technical papers, he was from the start acutely mindful of the nonverbal as an avenue to understanding. Reviewing the history of focused psychoanalytic concern with nonverbal behavior as communication, Wallerstein cited the early work of Wilhelm Reich on character armor, Felix Deutsch on what he called analytic posturology, and Meyer Zeligs who introduced the concept of acting in. He noted the work of researchers, such as Birdwhistell and Scheflen, who have published studies including film analyses of typical repetitive series of nonverbal behaviors occurring during phases of the psychotherapeutic hour and regularly associated with specific themes and affects. Jacobs, more recently, turned his attention to the posture, gestures, and bodily movements of the analyst as communicative cues to empathic understanding of the patient, as well as cues to blocking countertransferences in the analyst.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York City, December, 1976. Panelists: E. James Anthony, George F. Mahl; discussant, Manuel Furer.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Dialogue: Kleinian Theory Today

Hanna Segal

Hanna Segal

Hanna Segal  

I HAVE TO START WITH AN APOLOGY for my introduction to the dialogue being, of necessity, very inadequate.1 I find myself faced with a rather daunting task. Our theme is "Kleinian Analysis Today," but I know that I cannot take for granted that the readers have a sufficient grounding in basic Kleinian theory, say, as it was at the time of Mrs. Klein's death, for me to be able to introduce the discussion on new developments, so I will have to start by saying something about Klein's work, which again, in this brief account, cannot be done satisfactorily. Those of you who know Kleinian literature will find it very elementary, while for those of you not familiar with Kleinian concepts, the account will be too sketchy and too compressed.

I shall try to concentrate on a few basic concepts and then to indicate some guidelines to new developments, relying on the discussion to fill out and elaborate the themes.

In my view, Klein's work falls into three distinct phases. The first one, mainly based on child analysis, culminates in the early thirties with the publication of "The Importance of Symbol Formation in the Development of the Ego" in 1930 and The Psychoanalysis of Children in 1932. Then comes the formation of the concept of the depressive position and the bringing in of the whole concept of positions, and with it some departure from Freud's metapsychology, and finally, a formulation of the paranoid/schizoid position and the work on primitive envy.

1A reference list for the dialogue will be found on pp. 384-385.363

[[RunningHead]]

Further Observations on the Analyst's Cognitive Process

Paula Heimann M.D.

Paula Heimann

Paula Heimann, M.D.
32 Eamont Court
Eamont Street
London NW8 7DG
England

Paula Heimann  

IN RECENT TIMES INTEREST in the clinical situation has increasingly focused on the analyst, on what goes on in him and how he proceeds. Discussions of this kind have hitherto been largely confined to private study groups, seminars, and, of course, to supervision of students and young analysts.

Most of us learn probably more, and certainly more vividly, from exploring psychoanalytic technique than from erudite metapsychological expositions. Of course, both methods of guiding and checking our work are complementary to one another.

By establishing clarity about our own cognitive process we improve, above all, our own work. It is, in fact, part of our self-analysis. We also promote understanding between colleagues and also with workers in allied fields and with the interested public. In this way, I feel,we shall dispel misconceptions, illusions, and mysticism (the invidious return of religion) with regard to what we claim to be a scientific undertaking. That psychoanalysis is not only a science but also an art does not contradict the need for rigorous disciplined thinking. To move the "self-presentation" of the analyst at work from private to public exposition and debate means to show the strength and viability of psychoanalysis.

A slightly modified version of a paper read to the Canadian Psycho-Analytic Society on 11th December, 1975 in Montreal.

Submitted May 14, 1976

[[RunningHead]]

Long-Term Trends in Transference and Resistance: A Report on a Quantitative-Analytic Method Applied to Four Psychoanalyses

Harold Graff M.D. and Lester Luborsky Ph.D.

Lester Luborsky

Harold Graff, M.D.
Division of Psychoanalytic Studies Eastern Pennsylvania Psychiatric Institute
Henry Avenue and Abbottsford Road
Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19129
Lester Luborsky, Ph.D.

Lester Luborsky  

SUMMARY

Quantitative ratings of transference and resistance were made at each session during four psychoanalyses. Ratings were then compared with the treating psychoanalysts' clinical impressions. The two relatively successful analyses showed rising transference and diminishing resistance. The two relatively less successful analyses showed more parallel curves: the long, difficult analysis showed closely associated transference and resistance curves; the failure in analysis revealed a high resistance curve, even while transference increased.

[[RunningHead]]

Current Concepts of Normality

Arthur Valenstein M.D. and David Sachs M.D.

David Sachs

Arthur F. Valenstein, M.D.
David M. Sachs, M.D.
255 South 17 Street
Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19103

David Sachs  

Dr. Arthur F. Valenstein opened the panel by observing that normality has both a statistical and a normative definition which do not coincide. The statistical elevates what is into what ought to be and is not synonymous with mental health; the normative defines the normal in terms of what ought to be and is synonymous with mental health. He stressed the importance for the psychoanalyst of recognizing that the choice between the two definitions is not a matter of semantic curiosity because the differing attitudes which lie behind each definition affect all aspects of his work. Citing the work of Jahoda and Jones, he found support for his conclusion that the psychoanalytic concept of mental health is normative.

While not disputing the view that psychoanalysis began as a psychology of the abnormal in human behavior, Valenstein asserted that embedded within it from the beginning were concepts that later would be developed into a psychology of normality, including a normative theory of psychic development. Through quotations from Freud from 1900 to 1940 Valenstein documented his opinion that the study of the abnormal and the normal are areas of legitimate concern for psychoanalysts.

In "The Interpretation of Dreams" and "The Psychopathology of Everyday Life" Freud reported his discoveries that both symbolism in the formation of dreams and parapraxes could be considered phenomena that bridge the gap between normal mental life and the abnormal mental life of neurotics.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York City, December, 1976. Panelists: Samuel Abrams, Sibylle K. Escalona, Daniel Offer.

[[RunningHead]]

The Prototype of Preoedipal Reconstruction

Harold Blum M.D.

Harold Blum

Harold P. Blum, M.D.
23 The Hemlocks
Roslyn Estates, New York 11576

Harold Blum  

SUMMARY

Freud's first reported reconstruction was to the preoedipal period and referred to the psychological meaning and consequences of the birth and death of his younger brother, Julius. In

782

the historical development of psychoanalysis, preoedipal and oedipal reconstruction were simultaneously utilized, and Freud's thinking encompassed preoedipal influences with the Oedipus complex.

Reconstruction was one of the earliest discovered methods in psychoanalytic technique, reciprocally contributing to psychoanalytic theory, the uncovering of infantile amnesia, and awareness of the persistent influence of unconscious infantile conflict. Freud anticipated the importance of object relations in contemporary psychoanalytic theory before formulation of the libido theory. Reconstruction from dreams and screen memories converged in Freud's self-analysis in the discovery of the repression and revival of infantile object relations, leading to the concept of transference. Psychoanalysis was reconstructive, facilitating memory reorganization, and new ego synthesis.

Freud's self-analytic reconstruction concerning Julius, prototypical of preoedipal reconstruction, was to the proto-verbal anal developmental phase, and the rapprochement subphase of separation-individuation. His reconstruction is remarkably consistent with modern knowledge of developmental processes. The integration of psychoanalytic reconstruction and direct child observation promises a deeper understanding of ego development and disturbance, character formation, and preoedipal determinants of oedipal conflict and the infantile neurosis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pilgrimage: Action and Tradition in Self-Analysis

Warren Poland M.D.

Warren Poland

Warren S. Poland, M.D.
5225 Connecticut Avenue, N. W.
Washington, D. C. 20015

Warren Poland  

SUMMARY

Pilgrimages can provide a pattern of socially sanctioned behavior that serves as a context for new integration and

414

mastery of conflicts. They then provide a matrix in action for resolution of intrapsychic conflict, the act of pilgrimage facilitating the sublimatory processes leading to psychic change. These pilgrimages began as ambivalent acts of submission to parental imagoes which themselves had both rewarding ego-ideal and punitive-superego aspects. Despite resistances and often with what seemed to be counterphobic pressure, the pilgrims traveled to the sites, not of their own childhoods, but of their actual or idealized familial backgrounds. In the process of developing new solutions to their oedipal conflicts, personal growth was maintained within the context of traditional heritage.

Evidence of oedipal conflicts was manifestly clear in the pilgrims; early preoedipal issues were inferred. In the latter, the journey to the source manifested the regressive longing for union with the symbiotic good mother.

In the working through that was part of their travels, the pilgrims were able to work toward mastery of their unconscious conflicts within a sense of continuity of generations. The adaptive action involved in the pilgrimage described here is a special form of pilgrimage in the service of mastery and new ego synthesis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Defensive Alterations of Consciousness: Hypnoid States, Sleep, and the Dream

Robert Dickes M.D. and Daniel Papernik M.D.

Daniel Papernik

Robert Dickes, M.D.
Daniel S. Papernik, M.D.
Downstate Medical Center
Box 1203
450 Clarkson Avenue
Brooklyn, New York 11234

Daniel Papernik  

SUMMARY

The alteration in the state of consciousness, called the hypnoid state, is described. The reasons for selecting this term are given. Although this phenomenon occurs under the most ordinary circumstances and is ignored frequently, it functions as a significant ego defense, protecting the ego from unacceptable instinctual demands, and results in a pathological route to sleep. Hypnoid states are differentiated from "physiological sleepiness," from hypnosis, and from other alterations in the state of consciousness. Analytic material from the authors' cases as well as from a review of the literature demonstrates the ubiquitous nature of this phenomenon.

The authors postulate that the defensive function of altering one's state of consciousness occurs in sleeping as well as in waking life, that the superego initiates the development of the hypnoid state, and changes in consciousness occur in the ego to counter unacceptable sexual and aggressive impulses. For the defense to be successful, the hypnoid state must be of sufficient depth so that neither sexual nor aggressive responses are noted and access to the motor apparatus for direct expression is denied.

Attention is also directed to the management of the hypnoid state, which always functions as a resistance in treatment. Since even minor alterations can impede the progress of treatment, it is important to recognize its presence and to intervene at the appropriate time.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Energy Reconsidered—Discussion

Merton Gill M.D.

Merton Gill

Merton M. Gill, M.D.
P.O. Box 6998
Chicago, Illinois 60680

Merton Gill  

DISCUSSIONS OF PSYCHIC ENERGY SEEM LIKE DISCUSSIONS of religion and politics. They regularly recur, the disputants reiterate their familiar arguments, and no one's opinion is altered. The discussions are also usually conducted with the passion both indicating and befitting expressions of one's central epistemological perspectives, that is, what one feels the world is about. These are probably the reasons many people find a panel such as this a futile exercise if not downright boring.

I am not optimistic about changing anyone's opinion, but perhaps I can sharpen the statement of the nature of the disagreement. I propose that the concept of psychic energy is only a detail in conflicting over-all perspectives on what psychoanalysis is or should be. I am pleased to see that both Horowitz and Rosenblatt and Thickstun agree that we are discussing metapsychology as a whole, of which psychic energy is only one facet.

Psychoanalytic Dialogue: Kleinian Theory Today

Manuel Furer M.D.

Manuel Furer

Manuel Furer, M.D.
3 Lyndhurst Road
London NW3 5PX, England
166 East 93 Street
New York, New York 10028

Manuel Furer  

THE CAPTION "KLEINIAN THEORY TODAY" implies the possibility that Kleinian thought has evolved, that is, that the theory of today differs from the theory of the past. I gather that there is a first and second phase of Melanie Klein's work, the first having to do with particular vicissitudes of the destructive drive and with sadistic fantasies very early in life; the second, with the coordination of the psychic state of the infant with that of psychosis. A knowledge of the history of ideas enriches our understanding of them and will enlighten us in regard to the development of Melanie Klein's views.

The clearest succinct statement of Mrs. Klein's basic theoretical position I found in a panel discussion on the Mutual Influences in the Development of the Ego and the Id (Klein, 1952): "I have for many years held the view … that the ego functions from the beginning and that among its first activities are the defense against anxiety and the use of processes of introjection and projection… I have also repeatedly expressed the view that the ego establishes object relations from the first contacts with the external world… fantasies operate from the outset … and are the mental expression of the activity of both the life and death instincts" (pp. 51-52). With reference to this last, she says, "I differ from Freud in that I put forward the hypothesis that the primary cause of anxiety is the fear of annihilation, of death arising from the working of the death instinct within" (p. 51).

Notes on the Psychoanalytic Management of Archaic Transferences

John Gedo M.D.

John Gedo

John E. Gedo, M.D.
600 North McClurg Court
Chicago, Illinois 60611

John Gedo  

ONE OF THE MAIN AREAS OF RECENT EXPANSION in the scope of psychoanalysis has been in the direction of the systematic therapeutic handling of personality disturbances that have their genesis prior to the establishment of an infantile neurosis in the oedipal phase of development (cf. Kohut, 1971); (Kernberg, 1975); (Settlage, in preparation). In spite of general agreement about the capacity of such patients to experience intense archaic object relations within the analytic transference—experiences that presumably involve the repetition of certain crucial infantile transactions with the original caretakers—these syndromes have continued to be referred to by Freud's terminology of 1914, as "narcissistic." It seems clear, however, that in current usage the latter word has lost its original meaning—that of turning sexual cathexis away from others and onto oneself—without arriving at a consensus about what it refers to in phrases such as "narcissistic personality disturbance."

Although an exhaustive survey of the various points of view on this matter would take us too far afield from the limited aims of this clinical paper (but see Moore, 1975); (Stolorow, 1975);

Faculty, The Institute for Psychoanalysis, Chicago.

1One reason Kohut's work has proved to be quite confusing is that he alternates between this conception of narcissism as a variety of libido and another, quite inconsistent with it, according to which the term is to be understood metaphorically and the pathology of "narcissistic" disturbances is to be conceptualized in terms of self-cohesion. At the same time, Kohut has not as yet defined what he means by the term "self." In his last published discussion of the subject (1970), he confined the word to the designation of the mental representation of one's own person. It is not easy to imagine what a loss of cohesion means in these terms, beyond a report of subjective experience. At any rate, the metaphor central to this aspect of Kohut's writings is that of "empathy" (by which he seems to mean parental love, to start with, and the resultant development of self-love) as the cement holding the personality together.

Virginia Clower, in her introductory remarks, stated that the issues touched on by the presentations are on the frontier of psychoanalytic theory. They raise a host of questions about possible revisions in the basic tenets of psychoanalysis as a general psychology and about reconsideration of established concepts in theories of therapy. For example, the term "dyadic transference," derived from the developmental concept of object relations, makes it necessary to clarify our views on the entire psychoanalytic theory of object relations, the classical concept of transference neurosis, and accepted technical approaches to interpretation and working through.

Clower referred to the COPER Commission IX report in which the use of developmental concepts to expand classical theory and deepen analysis of adult neurotic patients is advocated. The suggestion that understanding both normal and pathological development might assist in the analysis of adult neurotic patients excited a great deal of interest, some controversy, and much discussion. Analysts were confronted with the fact that as yet there is no well-developed frame of reference within which a degree of structural deficit can be identified and dealt with in the transference neurosis of a neurotic adult. At this time, we have no substantial body of clinical experience demonstrating the use of developmental concepts in analyzing adults.

SUMMARY

It seems educationally advantageous to focus renewed attention on the different sets of propositions encompassed within the genetic point of view. Those assumptions fall into

423

two major classes: antecedents and transformations. Custom and usefulness seems to have brought these two together in the present classification of metapsychology. This re-emphasis of the differences between them appears warranted to further assert their distinctions and enhance the applicability of both.

Varieties of Oedipal Distortions in Severe Character Pathologies

Ernest Ticho Ph.D. and William Robbins M.D.

William Robbins

Ernest Ticho, Ph.D.
William S. Robbins, M.D.
10 Presidential Boulevard North
Bala Cynwyd, Pennsylvania 19004

William Robbins  

In his introductory remarks, Ernest Ticho observed that the Oedipus complex had been at the center of our psychology until the last decade when the increasing interest in pregenital developmental factors created the impression that the Oedipus complex had been displaced as the nuclear psychoanalytic developmental concept. The current emphasis on newer understandings and the interest it stimulates tend to exclude what was learned earlier; time is needed to arrive at a balanced view.

Ticho called attention to two papers written 45 years ago by Otto Fenichel: "The Pregenital Antecedents of the Oedipus Complex" (1941), and "The Specific Forms of the Oedipus Complex" (1931), which on rereading arouse a renewed respect for Fenichel's pioneering insights and also serve to remind us how far we have grown in conceptualizations and knowledge. Fenichel stated that the Oedipus complex is nuclear to the neuroses and to the unconscious of man generally, excepting from this only those extreme character malformations that resemble psychoses. He studied the preoedipal antecedents reflected in the oedipal organization and the libidinal development expressed in object relations. Fenichel pointed to the significance of environmental influences, but placed greater emphasis on biological and unconscious factors. He was fully aware of oedipal aspects in defense of preoedipal factors, which today we identify as premature oedipalization.

AS USUAL, GILL HAS BROADENED and deepened the discussion at hand, having raised the fundamental point of whether, in essence, we should concern ourselves with any of this at all. But for purposes of my discussion, I would like to say yes to the question, discuss the two papers, and then return to Gill's argument.

Dr. Horowitz reviews some of the fundamental economic concepts and the evidence on which they are based, pointing out how valuable they were in enlarging the scope of Freud's understanding. There is no doubt that this is so and also no doubt that, as he states, economic explanations pervade analytic contributions. This is not in itself an argument for their correctness or their usefulness at the present time. The question really is not whether they are used, but whether they are indispensable, especially in view of the complications and difficulties involved in energy theory. Horowitz cites the work of Mahler, Kohut, and Jacobson as examples of where the economic point of view has led to new understanding, but I feel that he has not established his case. In their work, important new clinical understanding has been offered, but seems not to depend necessarily on economic hypotheses. Although it is commonly said that the economic point of view is important in clinical understanding generally, it seems to me that just there it sees little essential use.
